title: Gabriel Rosenberg Dyspraxia/DCD Coverage Act
plain_summary: This bill asks the government to study how health insurance pays for treatments for dyspraxia, which is a condition that makes it hard for people to move and coordinate their body. The study will look at Medicare, Medicaid, and private insurance to see what treatments they cover and what problems people have getting care. The government has one year to finish the study and tell Congress what they found. The study will also include ideas for how to make insurance coverage better for people with this condition.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, i

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Gabriel Rosenberg Dyspraxia/DCD Coverage Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Study on Insurance Coverage of Dyspraxia/DCD
sections.text: Within one year after this law is passed, the Comptroller General of the United States (a government official who audits federal programs) must do a study and give Congress a report. The study will look at how well insurance covers treatments for dyspraxia/developmental coordination disorder (a condition that affects movement and coordination, also called DCD). The study will examine coverage under different types of health plans including employer group health plans, individual health insurance, government programs like Medicare and Medicaid, and federal employee health benefits. The report must include these eleven things. First, whether Medicare covers any treatments for this condition. Second, for each state, whether that state's Medicaid program covers any treatments for this condition. Third, whether other federal health programs not mentioned above cover treatments for this condition. Fourth, how much coverage is provided by employer group health plans and individual health insurance. Fifth, what types of treatments are usually covered when insurance does cover this condition. Sixth, any age limits that insurance companies set for covering these treatments. Seventh, things that make it hard to get coverage such as not having enough doctors who treat this condition. Eighth, whether health plans are following the law that requires them to cover mental health conditions equally with physical health conditions. Ninth, whether children with dyspraxia/DCD stop meeting the requirements for diagnosis when they become adults and therefore lose their insurance coverage. Tenth, recommendations about whether the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (the agency that runs these programs) should give guidance about covering treatments under Medicare and Medicaid. Eleventh, recommendations about how employer group health plans and individual health insurance could provide better coverage for treatments.
